title: Macramé photo frame
subtitle: A simple knotted frame to display a 15 x 10 cm photograph or postcard
excerpt: Knot a small macramé frame in 3 mm natural cotton cord, sized to hold a 15 x 10 cm photograph or postcard. The frame hangs flat against a wall and uses a cardboard backing to hold the photo in place.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from contemporary macramé practice. Knotted photo frames are a current British macramé beginner project; the flat-panel construction with cardboard backing follows standard macramé frame conventions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The core macramé knot: two working cords tied around two filler cords, first left over right, then right over left.
A mounting knot used to attach folded cords to a dowel or ring. The folded cord loop passes over the dowel and the two tails are pulled through the loop and down.
A wrapping knot in which one cord is wound repeatedly around a bundle of other cords to draw them together. Used at the top of macramé pieces to bundle hanging cords.

content.text: A macramé photo frame is built as a flat rectangular panel with a cut-out centre window. The frame is knotted in
at the top bundles the hanging cords; the photo slots between the knotted front panel and a cardboard backing piece glued to the reverse.

Cut 12 lengths of cord, each 100 cm long. Fold each in half and mount on the dowel using lark's head knots, giving 24 working cords. Space the knots evenly across the 16 cm working width of the dowel.

Tie 2 rows of square knots across all 24 cords, then 1 row of alternating square knots, then 2 more rows of standard square knots. This 5-row section forms the top border of the frame, about 4 cm deep.
Step 3: Create the side borders and central opening

To create the photo window, work the next 10 cm on the left 4 cords only (2 square knots per row, working downward), and on the right 4 cords only, leaving the central 16 cords unworked. These unworked central cords form the space behind which the photo will sit.

Bring all 24 cords back together and tie 2 rows of square knots across the full width, then 1 row of alternating square knots, then 2 more standard rows to close the bottom border. Trim the cord ends to about 3 cm below the last knot.
Step 5: Attach the backing and insert the photo

Cut a cardboard backing piece to 17 x 12 cm. Apply craft glue to the top and bottom frame borders and press the cardboard backing in place, leaving the central window open for the photo to slide in from the side. Allow to dry fully before inserting the photo.

items.problem: The central window is too small for the photo
items.cause: The side borders were worked with too many knots, leaving less open space than intended
items.fix: Work only 2 cords per side for the border sections rather than 4, or reduce the number of knots per side to widen the central opening.

items.problem: The frame does not hang flat against the wall
items.cause: The cord tension was uneven across the panel, causing the frame to curve
items.fix: Spray lightly with starch water and press flat under a heavy book while damp to encourage a flat hang.
